evidence_quotes.quote: started using "Games as a Service" as an excuse to essentially delete games.
they also like to claim that you do not own the games, but only a licence to access the games and thus that absolves them of all problems in that regard.

A product that you paid for, and own is suddenly simply unusable, and the time prior to this happening is mostly random, and heavily dependant on whether or not the developper thinks the game is worth supporting.

paragraphs.text: Hi, As a regular buyer and consumer of digital goods, in particular video games, I would like to point out that the video games industry has been doing practices that are quite often murky at best, and can be argued at times to be literal theft. In essence, video games used to be sold on cartridges and discs a not so long time ago. During these times, the games were essentially expected to last forever, outside of the physical medium getting damaged itself. Nowadays, discs are almost useless outside of launching games because most games are larger than what a DvD can hold, and games are also available entirely in a digital way ,via online stores. One would assume these would last forever, being files that can be downloaded again should they be corrupted. But the industry has taken a liking to creating a planed obsolessence of video games. On top of their usual predatory ways of including microtransactions which is another can of worms, they have for over a decade now, started using "Games as a Service" as an excuse to essentially delete games. The way this works in theory is that the game requires an online connection to the developper, or publisher's servers in order to be played. This is supposedly to ensure that the game is not tampered with etc. This is somewhat logical for a multiplayer game, but is also used on singleplayer games. And while this is all well and good, the problem arrises when the developper or publisher decides to simply shut down the server that checks your connection. In most cases, the game becomes a digital brick because they do not remove this requirement. A product that you paid for, and own is suddenly simply unusable, and the time prior to this happening is mostly random, and heavily dependant on whether or not the developper or publisher thinks the game is worth supporting. This issue also happens with online stores support ending by itself. Imagine if you were to buy a car, and the car manufacturer suddenly decided that you could not start your car anymore because they stopped making that car model. You would be stuck with a car that is useless. Essentially a brick. The video game issue I am talking about is basically the same thing. They also like to claim that you do not own the games, but only a licence to access the games and thus that absolves them of all problems in that regard. But if you buy a song or album, or a movie, even online, while you do not own the rights to the movie ,you own the files of that movie, and may use them as you please so long as it is for personnal use. So why would this be any different for video games, you did buy a copy of the game, even digitally. Whether or not you believe video games are a form of art, I do not think you would like to see music, books or movies disappear. The same goes for me for video games, I and many others believe they should be preserved, and kept playable for future generations. On that note, the Stop Destroying Games initiative has reached over 1.4 million signatures and seeks to establish rules regarding the destruction of video games, and proves that citizens of the EU want their games to not be destroyed. Not by asking the developper or publisher to support that game forever as the European Games Lobby claims, but to remove the requirement to connect to their servers when they are done supporting the game and leave the game in a playable state at a bare minimum. A thing that developpers used to do back then, many old games that are not supported anymore still work, sometimes with a community made patch. These developpers did not have to pay for the game still being playable. Many games have already disappeared this way, including most recently, The Crew, which Ubisoft has completely revoked the access to, and want people to delete their copy. The game is now a digital brick and all the owners of the game essentially have had the game stolen from them. I thank you for your consideration of this issue. Kind regards.
